file_name: Gupta et al. - 2025 - FedMed-XAI a collaborative and trustworthy framework for skin cancer detection using federated lear.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning (CNNs) and federated learning (FedProx) for skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dataset, integrated with explainable AI (Grad-CAM).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper introduces a novel framework called FedMed-XAI, describes its methodology (integrating FedProx, Grad-CAM, and CNNs), details the experimental setup (using the HAM10000 dataset across 20 clients), and reports original experimental results and evaluation metrics. It is not a narrative or systematic review of prior literature, although it includes a related work section.

title: FedMed‑XAI: a collaborative and trustworthy framework for skin cancer detection using federated learning and explainable AI
brief_summary: This study introduces FedMed-XAI, a collaborative framework integrating federated learning and explainable AI to address privacy and interpretability challenges in skin cancer detection. The authors utilized the HAM10000 dataset, deploying the system across 20 decentralized clients over 200 communication rounds to train Convolutional Neural Networks using the FedProx algorithm. To enhance trustworthiness, the framework incorporates Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) to generate visual explanations for model predictions without sharing raw patient data. The experimental setup leveraged high-performance computing resources to manage the scalability and communication overhead inherent in the distributed training process. The results demonstrate that the proposed approach successfully achieves both privacy preservation and model interpretability, offering a scalable solution for real-world medical applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on a binary or multi-class classification task for skin cancer detection using the HAM10000 dataset. While the HAM10000 dataset contains 7 specific lesion classes (melanoma, melanocytic nevi, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratoses, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesions), the provided text does not explicitly list these specific classes as the distinct labels used in the FedMed-XAI experiments, referring generally to 'skin cancer classification' and 'skin lesions'.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The framework uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to predict discrete diagnostic categories (skin lesions/cancer). Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F1-measure, which are standard for classification tasks. The text explicitly mentions 'skin cancer classification' and determining if a lesion is benign or malignant.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive library of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions gathered from various populations, made available for the Kaggle detection challenge competition. It contains 10,015 published dermatoscopic images, each measuring 600 × 450 pixels.

use.training: Used for federated learning training across 20 decentralized clients. The training set (80% of data) was partitioned into non-overlapping client-specific subsets using StratifiedKFold, with each client receiving roughly 500 images. Data augmentation (random horizontal flipping, rotation, color jittering) and stratified resampling were applied to mitigate class imbalance.
use.testing: Used for testing (20% of data). Only resizing and normalization were applied to the test set.
use.other: Used for demographic analysis (age and localization heatmaps) and as the source for simulating decentralized data distribution.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The study focuses on performance evaluation using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F1-measure, and provides a theoretical convergence proof for the FedMed-XAI framework, but does not perform statistical hypothesis testing on the experimental results.

FedProxwithXAI.note: Outperformed standard FedProx; improved convergence and lower loss compared to non-XAI counterparts

FedAvgwithXAI.note: Outperformed standard FedAvg; exhibited more stable learning and improved accuracy

FedProx.note: Exhibited higher variance and slower convergence compared to FedMed-XAI

FedAvg.note: Exhibited slower convergence and higher variability in accuracy

Centralized Model.note: Achieved highest accuracy early in training and maintained consistently superior performance across all rounds

HAM10000.notes: The framework achieved an overall accuracy of 91.2% in 200 rounds. Each lesion category achieved an AUC of 1.00. Class-specific performance varied due to dataset imbalance: Melanocytic nevi (NV) showed high recall (0.94) but lower specificity (0.18). Classes with fewer samples (AKIEC, DF, VASC) exhibited greater specificity but significantly lower recall and F1 scores. FedMed-XAI consistently achieved the lowest loss across rounds, closely approaching centralized performance.

architecture_used.input_size: 600x450 pixels (resized to 150x100 for the first convolutional layer based on output dimensions)

classification_or_output_head.activation: Not explicitly stated for final layer, but output is 7 classes

conclusions: The proposed FedMed-XAI framework successfully integrates federated learning (FedProx) and explainable AI (Grad-CAM) to achieve both privacy preservation and model interpretability for skin cancer detection.
The framework achieved an overall classification accuracy of 91.2% and per-class ROC-AUC scores of 1.00 on the HAM10000 dataset across 20 decentralized clients over 200 communication rounds.
FedMed-XAI demonstrated superior convergence and lower loss compared to standard FedProx and FedAvg baselines, while closely approaching the performance of a centralized model.
The inclusion of Grad-CAM regularization in the federated learning process was theoretically proven to preserve L-smoothness and bounded gradient properties, ensuring algorithmic convergence.

Class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset significantly impacted performance, with melanocytic nevi (NV) showing high recall (0.94) but low specificity (0.18), while rare classes (AKIEC, DF, VASC) exhibited high specificity but lower recall and F1 scores.
The study acknowledges that while the framework addresses privacy and interpretability, future work should address class imbalance through strategies such as class-weighted loss functions.
The use of high-performance computing (HPC) resources was essential to manage the scalability, parallel processing, and communication overhead inherent in the distributed training of 20 clients.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., 6705 samples for NV vs. 115 for DF), leading to skewed performance metrics where rare classes exhibit high specificity but low recall.

The framework was evaluated in a simulated federated environment with 20 clients; it does not report results from real-world clinical deployment or external validation on independent datasets, limiting generalizability claims.

Per-class ROC-AUC scores were reported as 1.00, indicating perfect separability, which is unusually high for skin cancer classification tasks and may suggest overfitting or data leakage issues not fully addressed in the text.

The paper acknowledges that class imbalance negatively impacts performance and suggests future work on class-weighted loss functions, indicating a current limitation in handling minority classes effectively.

While the framework claims privacy preservation via Federated Learning, the use of the publicly available HAM10000 dataset for all clients raises questions about the realism of the privacy simulation and potential data overlap in a real-world setting.

The study utilizes high-performance computing (HPC) resources to manage scalability, which may limit the accessibility and deployment feasibility of the framework in resource-constrained clinical environments.
